title: The $0 collect() That Ate the Driver
author: Anonymous Engineer
tldr: Called .collect() on a 2TB DataFrame thinking it was a sample. Driver OOMed, took down the entire shared cluster.
story: We had a data quality check job running nightly. Junior dev added a quick sanity check — df.collect() — to 'just see the first few rows'. Except this was on the full unfiltered raw events table. 2TB. The driver tried to pull all of it into the JVM heap, hit 64GB limit in about 90 seconds, and crashed. Because it was a shared cluster, it took down 4 other production jobs mid-run. Incident lasted 3 hours. The fix: df.show(20) or df.limit(20).collect(). Cost us a Monday morning.
lesson: Never call .collect() on a DataFrame unless you have explicitly limited it first. Use .show(), .take(n), or .limit(n).collect() instead. Set spark.driver.maxResultSize to a safe cap.

title: The Broadcast Join That Wasn't Small
author: Senior DE, FAANG
tldr: Auto-broadcast kicked in on a 'dimension' table that had grown to 8GB overnight. Every executor OOMed simultaneously.
story: We had a dimension table join that had always been under 100MB — safely within the broadcast threshold. One night a batch job ran without a filter predicate due to a config bug and loaded the full historical snapshot: 8GB. Spark's auto-broadcast (spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold) happily decided to broadcast it. 120 executors all tried to hold 8GB in memory simultaneously. Every single one OOMed. The job failed silently at 2am, and downstream dashboards showed stale data until 10am when someone noticed.
lesson: Set spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold to a conservatively low value (10MB). Explicitly hint joins you intend to broadcast with broadcast(). Add row-count checks to dimension table loads.

title: groupByKey: The OOM Generator
author: Anonymous
tldr: Used groupByKey on a high-cardinality user_id field. 600M users × average 40 events each materialised entirely in executor memory.
story: Migrating a Hadoop job to Spark, we translated groupBy().mapValues() directly into groupByKey().mapValues(). It worked fine in testing on a 1% sample. In production, groupByKey materialises ALL values for each key into a single in-memory iterator before passing it to your function. With 600M users averaging 40 events each, individual partitions were holding 2-3GB of data per key group. Executors started OOMing one by one. The job took 14 hours before finally dying.
lesson: Replace groupByKey with reduceByKey, aggregateByKey, or combineByKey wherever possible. These combine locally before shuffling. For complex aggregations in DataFrames use groupBy().agg() — Spark handles it efficiently under the hood.

title: How I Accidentally Spent £28k in a Weekend
author: Data Engineer, London agency
tldr: Left a Databricks all-purpose cluster running over a long weekend instead of terminating it. Auto-termination was off.
story: Friday afternoon, demoing a new pipeline to a client on Databricks. Spun up an all-purpose cluster — 32 nodes, i3.4xlarge, because the client wanted to see 'production scale'. Demo went great. Got an early train, completely forgot to terminate the cluster. Auto-termination: disabled (I'd turned it off for the demo so it wouldn't time out mid-presentation). Monday morning, Slack message from our AWS account owner: 'Did you mean to spend £28k this weekend?' The cluster ran for 63 hours straight. Zero workloads executed.
lesson: Always set cluster auto-termination, even in demos — 30 minutes is fine, you can always restart. Set up billing alerts at 20%, 50%, 80% of monthly budget. Never use all-purpose clusters for anything you can run on job clusters.

title: The Recursive S3 List That Ran for a Week
author: Anonymous
tldr: A misconfigured Glue crawler recursively listed a prefix containing 11 years of event data. S3 LIST requests billed at $0.005 per 1000.
story: Set up a Glue crawler to catalogue a new data lake prefix. Accidentally pointed it at s3://our-bucket/ instead of s3://our-bucket/new-events/. The bucket root contained 11 years of event data — roughly 400 million objects. The crawler spent 6 days doing recursive LIST operations across the entire bucket before someone noticed it was still running. AWS bill for that month had a $4,200 line item for S3 API requests alone.
lesson: Always scope crawlers to the most specific prefix possible. Set a max runtime on Glue crawlers. Enable S3 bucket request metrics so you get alerted on unusual LIST volumes. Consider AWS Config rules that alert on runaway crawler jobs.

title: Spot Interruption + No Checkpointing = 72h of Wasted Compute
author: ML Engineer turned DE
tldr: Long-running Spark Streaming job on 100% spot instances. No checkpointing. Spot interrupted after 18h. Job restarted from scratch 4 times.
story: Running a feature engineering pipeline on EMR spot instances to save costs. Job took ~18 hours on the spot fleet we could afford. No checkpointing configured because 'we'll add it later'. Spot market spiked and our fleet got interrupted at hour 18. Job restarted from zero. This happened 4 times over 3 days. Each failed run cost roughly $2,100. By the time we added checkpointing and switched the last 20% of the job to on-demand instances, we'd burned $8,500 to do a job that should have cost $2,100 once.
lesson: If your job runs >2 hours, checkpointing is not optional. Mix spot (80%) + on-demand (20%) for the driver and critical executors. Use EMR managed scaling with a minimum on-demand base capacity.

title: The Silent Null That Poisoned 6 Months of Reports
author: Data Engineer, healthcare
tldr: A LEFT JOIN silently introduced NULLs into a revenue metric column. No null checks downstream. Finance reports were wrong for 6 months.
story: Migrated a revenue aggregation pipeline from SQL Server to Spark. The original query used an INNER JOIN. I rewrote it as a LEFT JOIN to 'preserve all records'. The join key had ~3% non-matches. Spark happily produced NULLs for the revenue column on those rows. SUM(revenue) in SQL ignores NULLs, so totals were silently understated by ~3%. No tests, no null checks, no data quality alerts. Finance noticed the discrepancy 6 months later when auditing against a third-party system. We had to reprocess and reissue 6 months of reports.
lesson: Add explicit null checks after every join. Use df.filter(col('key_column').isNull()) to assert zero nulls on columns that should never be null. Tools like Great Expectations or dbt tests exist precisely for this.

title: Idempotency? Never Heard of Her.
author: Anonymous Senior Engineer
tldr: Pipeline retried on failure and wrote duplicate rows. No deduplication. 11M duplicate transactions ended up in the data warehouse.
story: ETL job failed at step 3 of 5 due to a transient network error. Airflow retried the full DAG from step 1 (not from the failure point). Steps 1 and 2 each wrote to an append-mode Delta table. They ran again without issue. By the time we noticed, 11 million transaction rows had been written twice. The warehouse FACT_TRANSACTIONS table had 22M rows when it should have had 11M. Downstream revenue reports were exactly double. The CEO's weekly dashboard showed a 100% revenue spike on a Tuesday.
lesson: Every write step must be idempotent. Use Delta Lake MERGE or INSERT OVERWRITE partition-by-partition. Never use append mode for pipeline outputs unless you have explicit deduplication. Design Airflow DAGs to retry from the failed task, not from start.

title: The Timezone That Ate New Year's Eve
author: Anonymous
tldr: Timestamps stored without timezone info. Pipeline assumed UTC. Data was in US/Eastern. Midnight rollover duplicated an hour of events at New Year.
story: Event data from a US app was ingested as Unix timestamps and converted to datetime during processing. The pipeline assumed UTC. The app server was running on US/Eastern. This 5-hour offset meant every day's partition was slightly wrong, but within noise. On New Year's Eve, the 1-hour DST rollback caused events between 23:00-24:00 EST to appear twice when processed in UTC. The New Year traffic spike looked like 2x normal on every dashboard. The on-call engineer spent 4 hours on New Year's Day investigating a non-existent traffic anomaly.
lesson: Always store timestamps in UTC at the source. Validate timezone assumptions in your schema. Add a timezone column alongside the timestamp if the source can be in multiple zones. Write a unit test that processes a DST-boundary event.

title: The Schema Evolution That Wasn't
author: Staff Engineer, fintech
tldr: Upstream team added a column to the Kafka event schema. No schema registry. Spark job reading with a hardcoded schema silently dropped the new column for 3 weeks.
story: We owned the downstream Spark Structured Streaming job. The upstream team owned the Kafka producer. They added a new boolean field is_fraud_flagged to the event schema. Great feature. They told someone who told someone who forgot to tell us. Our job used spark.read.schema(hardcodedSchema). Spark's default behaviour on unknown columns: drop them silently. For 3 weeks we processed 40M events per day, all with is_fraud_flagged = null. The fraud detection model downstream got null features. Model degraded gradually. Took a model performance review to trace it back.
lesson: Use a Schema Registry (Confluent or AWS Glue). Enable schema-on-read with mergeSchema=True in Delta/Parquet. Set up alerting when new columns appear in source data. Treat upstream schema changes as a breaking change contract that requires notification.

title: How a String 'null' Broke the Entire Lakehouse
author: Anonymous DE
tldr: Source system sent the literal string 'null' instead of SQL NULL. Downstream joins all failed silently because 'null' != NULL.
story: A third-party logistics API started returning the string 'null' (4 characters: n, u, l, l) for missing values instead of an actual JSON null. Our ingestion job cast it to a string column without issue. The problem surfaced 2 days later when joins to the reference table on that column started returning zero matches. customer_id = 'null' will never match customer_id = NULL in SQL semantics. Every record with a missing customer silently dropped out of all downstream joins. We lost 2 days of customer-level metrics before anyone noticed.
lesson: Add a post-ingestion check that scans for string literals that look like null values: 'null', 'NULL', 'None', 'NaN', 'N/A', 'na', ''. Replace them with actual SQL NULLs or a sentinel value. Document which columns allow null and enforce it with schema validation.
